user_feedback: #2 User feedback: Ability to customize or tweak the generated media content based on teacher feedback
chenyu_and_related: Chenyu's takeaway: This is a feature request so that teachers can modify the media content. Basically, we should allow teachers to further interact with the generated media instead of just being given whatever is generated. I think there are two things we can do: 1. The immediate approach is to have a regenerate button so the teacher can regenerate the video if they are not satisfied with it. 2. A more advanced feature is to allow teachers to write their own custom prompts so the videos can include that specific input. Revision plan:  (add regenerate button/teacher prompt in future)

user_feedback: #4 User feedback: Other than the recommended strategy, if teacher select the other strategies, there should be similar elaborations to articulate this strategy, as the recommended one.
chenyu_and_related: Chenyu's takeaway: I don't think this user's feedback makes sense. Regarding the suggestion to articulate this strategy, perhaps the tester means we need to elaborate on what the strategy is. As a next step for the feedback, we can basically explain what each strategy. When teacher selects other non-recommended strategies, we explain what the strategy is and why it’s not appropriate.

user_feedback: #5 User feedback: From a teacher perspective, the distinctions among the four strategies are not entirely clear for every teacher. Is there a formal definition or framework that explains how each strategy is defined and differentiated? From a research perspective, the student questionnaire seems to prompt students to draw on their own experiences to explain their thinking. Given this design, it is not surprising that experience bridging shows the highest percentage. I’m wondering whether this distribution reflects the intended construct being measured, or if it is influenced by how the pre-questions are framed. Is the questionnaire designed this way intentionally, or is the example shaping students’ responses?
chenyu_and_related: Chenyu's takeaway: This is great feedback. I guess the next step is to figure out how distinct those four strategies are, whether they have overlaps or if they are totally distinct. We need to make sure the questions are not biased. Even for the handpicked ones, the quizzes, or our two questions, we must ensure those questions do not lead to specific strategies. Based on our current assumption, they don't, but we need to test this. Then, we may need to run many simulated student responses to see their distribution in order to understand how they map to different strategies. Confirmation bias is the cognitive tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information that confirms one's pre-existing beliefs or hypotheses. Revision: more explanation of teacher self-selected strategy

user_feedback: #18 User feedback: Most critically, setting aside the quality of the images/videos (e.g., coherence, multimedia design), the generated content currently does not align with the intended engagement strategies. This should be refined further. The “Community Gallery” should also be reformatted so users can easily identify which lesson/topic each artifact was created for, including which ones they personally created. "Save" function is currently for each image/video. But, it should save the entire learning materials as one file, such as word, slides, html, etc.
chenyu_and_related: Chenyu's takeaway: Regarding the save functionality, we have two options: saving either a single material or the entire set of learning materials. First, we need to clarify that we do not generate the learning materials themselves; it is just the images or the videos. If users want to save other types of content, I think the options need to be more granular. Alternatively, we could make this a complete data dump where we provide everything to the teachers, which could also be a valid approach. For the community gallery, we can certainly include the lesson topic. This is currently included if you click on the image in the zoomed version. Regarding the feedback that the generated content does not align with the intended engagement strategies: I do not think that is accurate. The image is basically an illustration of the experiment, so it does not tie to a specific strategy. At the very least, you cannot determine the strategy from the image alone.

user_feedback: #19 User feedback: as a teacher, i may wonder why one of the strategies is recommended. when multiple strategies are recommended, how could i personalize my teaching? Since there is no demo for student interface, i don't know how recommended learning materials benefit students.
chenyu_and_related: Chenyu's takeaway: This is not a feature request; rather, we might need to think about the user experience here. We need to clarify that this material is for teachers to use in their class. Maybe we need to add a section for teachers regarding how they can use it. We need to manage the expectations of the videos.

user_feedback: #23 User feedback: I hope the generated materials can be more aligned with the strategies, such as the cognitive conflict. I found that the materials generated are similar to the experience bridging.
chenyu_and_related: Chenyu's takeaway: Based on the feedback, my primary takeaways are that we need to adjust how images and videos are generated and clarify the goals for how teachers can use these materials. Specifically regarding the video content: 1.  Current limitations Right now, we only generate six-second videos. This may not provide enough time for viewers to see the end results of the experiments. 2.  Potential adjustments If we offer longer videos, it might enable different use cases. We need to decide on the pedagogical approach: (a) Should we show only the start to make students think? (b) Should we show the end results and ask for their observations? There are several different approaches we can take here.
